title: MoE: Activating Subsets
concept: A Mixture of Experts (MoE) model contains multiple small 'expert' networks (e.g. 8 experts), activating only a few (e.g. 2 experts) per token.

exercises.prompt: What is the main idea behind a Mixture of Experts (MoE) model?

options.text: It contains multiple 'expert' networks, activating only a few per token.
It combines all human experts into a single training database.
It forces AI models to write code using only one programming language.

exercises.explain: An MoE model consists of multiple small networks called 'experts', activating only a small subset of them at any time to process input efficiently.

cards.title: Smart Routing
cards.body: AI models are getting massive, but running them takes a huge amount of electricity and time. What if we could build a giant model, but only use a tiny fraction of it at any given moment? Enter Mixture of Experts!

cards.title: What is MoE?
cards.body: A Mixture of Experts (MoE) model is an AI brain divided into many small specialist networks called 'experts'. Instead of running the whole brain for every word, it only wakes up a few specific experts (like 2 out of 8) that are best suited for the task.

cards.title: The Specialist Hospital
cards.body: Imagine a giant hospital. Instead of every doctor treating every single patient, a receptionist sends you to only the two specialists who know about your specific problem. The other doctors keep working on other things, saving everyone's time!

cards.title: MoE in Action
cards.body: When you type the word 'French', the router instantly sends this word to the 'Language Translation' expert and the 'European History' expert. The other six experts (like Math or Coding) stay asleep.

cards.title: Myth: Bigger Means Slower
cards.body: People think MoE models must run all of their parameters for every single query. In reality, because only a few experts are active at a time, the model runs incredibly fast while still holding massive amounts of knowledge.

cards.title: Dense vs. MoE
cards.body: Let's compare a traditional Dense Model where the entire network works on everything, versus a Mixture of Experts model that selectively activates specialists.

left.text: Every single parameter is active and calculating for every token. Heavy and slow.

right.text: Only 2 out of 8 experts are active per token. Lightweight, fast, and smart.

exercises.prompt: How does a Mixture of Experts (MoE) model save computing power?

options.text: Every single expert runs at the same time for every word
Only a small subset of experts are activated for each word
Experts are randomly deleted to save space on your computer
The model converts text into images before processing

exercises.explain: By only activating a subset of experts (like 2 out of 8) for each token, MoE models get the intelligence of a huge model with the speed of a smaller one.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a Mixture of Experts (MoE) model, every expert must process every token.
exercises.explain: MoE models only activate a subset of experts for each word, allowing the other experts to remain inactive and save computing power.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of MoE routing.
exercises.sentence: The MoE model uses a smart classifier called a router to send each token to the correct experts.
exercises.explain: The router acts like a smart traffic cop, directing incoming tokens to the most relevant expert networks.

exercises.prompt: Which system is demonstrating a Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture?

options.text: System A: Runs the entire 100-billion parameter network for every single word.
System B: Directs the word 'code' to Expert 3 and Expert 7, leaving the other 6 experts inactive.
System C: Translates the word into Spanish and then processes it using a single CPU.

exercises.explain: System A behaves like a standard dense transformer (all parts work on every word). System B shows the classic MoE behavior of routing tokens to a selected subset of expert networks. System C is just a simple translator.

exercises.prompt: If an MoE model has 16 total experts but only activates 2 experts per word, what actually happens when you type a prompt?

options.text: All 16 experts process every single word simultaneously
Only 2 experts are activated per word, keeping costs low
The model randomly shuts down half of its brain to save energy
The model merges all 16 experts into one single giant network

exercises.explain: Mixture of Experts models only activate a tiny subset of experts for each token. This means we get the power of a massive model with the speed and computational cost of a much smaller one.
